---
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, mytheme.scss]
    mermaid:
      theme: "default"
      format: "svg"   
    css: styles.css
    footer: "EPHI / NDMC Hackathon — 2026"
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    auto-stretch: true
    navigation-mode: linear
    controls: true
    controls-layout: edges
    slide-number: c/t
    hash: true
    code-copy: hover
    code-link: true
    code-overflow: scroll
    embed-resources: false
# Force initialization of layout tools
keep-md: true
embed-resources: true
execute:
  eval: false
  echo: true
---

<div style="text-align: center;">

<h1 style="font-size: 2.5em; margin: 1rem 0; color: #2c3e50;">
Reproducibility and Distributed Version Control<br>
</h1>


<div style="margin-top: 3rem;">
  <p style="font-size: 1.4em; color: #2c2c2c; margin: 0; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: 2px;">
 <br> </br>
    Mesfin D. (PhD)
     <br> </br>
  </p>
  <p style="font-size: 1.1em; color: #666; margin-top: 0.6rem;">
    <br> </br>
    July, 2026
  <br> </br>
  Hackathon Theme: Bridging Modeling & Policy to Solve Public Health Challenges
  <br> </br>
  </p>
</div>

</div>

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="100%"}

![](fig/ephi_logo.png){width="10%"}

:::
::::



## What is Version Control?

- A **version control system (VCS)** tracks changes to files over time.
- It allows you to:
  - Recover older versions (like a time machine).
  - See *who* changed *what* and *why* (commit messages).
  - Work on multiple features simultaneously (branches).
  - Merge changes from different people.
- Without VCS, we rely on manual backups – chaotic.


> Git is the most popular modern VCS – we will use it throughout.

---

## Git vs GitHub – The Difference

| **Git** | **GitHub** |
|---------|------------|
| A **command-line tool** that runs locally on your computer | A **cloud platform** that hosts Git repositories |
| Manages commits, branches, and history | Provides a web UI, Issues, Projects, and Actions |
| Works entirely offline | Requires internet for collaboration |

> **Analogy:** Git is the engine; GitHub is the garage where you store and share your car.

---

## Distributed Version Control Systems (DVCS)

- **Centralised** (e.g., SVN): a single server holds all history – single point of failure.
- **Distributed** (Git): every developer has a **full copy** of the repository.
  - Work offline, commit locally.
  - Sync with any other copy when online.

> **Key takeaway:** Git is distributed – every laptop is a full backup.

---

## Installing Git (All Operating Systems)

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Linux (Ubuntu/Debian)
```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install git-all -y
git --version
```

### macOS
```bash
# via Homebrew (recommended)
brew install git
# or use the built-in xcode-select stub
git --version
```

### Windows
1. Download from [git-scm.com](https://git-scm.com/download/win)
2. Run the installer (keep default settings – **Git Bash** is essential)
3. Open **Git Bash** and verify:
```bash
git --version
```
:::

---

## Installing uv – Python Environment Manager (All OS)

`uv` is a fast Python package installer and resolver. Install it before using it in your projects.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Linux (Ubuntu/Debian)
```bash
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
# Alternatively, use wget:
wget -qO- https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
```

### macOS
```bash
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
# Or via Homebrew:
brew install uv
```

### Windows
```bash
# Using PowerShell (run as Administrator):
powershell -c "irm https://astral.sh/uv/install.ps1 | iex"
```
Alternatively, download the latest `uv.exe` from [astral.sh/uv](https://astral.sh/uv) and add it to your PATH.
:::

> After installation, verify with `uv --version`. The `uv` command should be available in your terminal.

---

## Installing Project Generators

### Python – CCDS 

CCDS is a cross‑project utility, so the **official recommendation** is to install it with `pipx`.

```bash
# 1. Install pipx (if not already installed)
python3 -m pip install --user pipx
python3 -m pipx ensurepath

# 2. Install CCDS via pipx (recommended)
pipx install cookiecutter-data-science
```

> **Note:** CCDS v2 requires Python 3.9+. The `ccds` command will now be available globally.

### R – Cookiecutter (Standard)

Cookiecutter itself is a Python package. You can install it with `pip` (it does not need to be isolated like CCDS).

```bash
pip install cookiecutter
```

Alternatively, if you prefer `pipx`:
```bash
pipx install cookiecutter
```

> **Note:** The AP R template (`associatedpress/cookiecutter-r-project`) is a standard Cookiecutter template and works with either installation method.

---

## First-Time Git Configuration

Set your **identity** – this is attached to every commit:

```bash
git config --global user.name "Your Full Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@ephi.gov.et"
git config --global core.editor "nano -w"   # or your favourite editor
git config --global init.defaultBranch main
```

Check settings:
```bash
git config --list --global
```

> **Important:** Use the same email as your GitHub account.

---

## Local Git Basics – The Three Areas

- **Working Directory** – the files you see and edit.
- **Staging Area (Index)** – a temporary holding zone for changes you plan to commit.
- **Repository (`.git/`)** – the hidden folder that stores all commits and history.

**Workflow:**
1. Modify files in the Working Directory.
2. Stage them with `git add`.
3. Commit the staged snapshot with `git commit`.

---

## The Three File States

1. **Modified** – changed but not yet staged.
2. **Staged** – marked to be included in the next commit.
3. **Committed** – safely stored in the repository.

Use `git status` to see which state each file is in.

---

## Creating a Project with CCDS (Python) or Cookiecutter (R)

**CCDS** generates a complete project structure from a template. We will use the standard **Cookiecutter Data Science** template for Python (via the `ccds` command) and the **Associated Press R template** for R. The interactive prompts let you customise your project name, description, and dependencies.

Below are the steps for each challenge. **Choose your challenge tab**, then select **Python** or **R** and follow the terminal interaction. The answers shown are tailored to that challenge – adapt as needed.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Challenge 1 – Zero‑dose Burden

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### Python Project
```bash
# Run the template (no need to create a directory first – CCDS does it for you)
ccds
```

**Terminal interaction** (answer each prompt):
```
You've downloaded /Users/username/.cookiecutters/cookiecutter-data-science before.
Is it okay to delete and re-download it? [y/n] (y): y
project_name (project_name): zero-dose-burden
repo_name (zero-dose-burden): zero-dose-burden
module_name (zero_dose_burden): zero_dose_burden
author_name (Your name (or your organization/company/team)): NDMC-Alpha
description (A short description of the project.): Estimate district-level zero-dose burden by integrating household surveys and routine immunisation data.
python_version_number (3.10): 3.10
Select dataset_storage:
1 - none
2 - azure
3 - s3
4 - gcs
Choose from [1/2/3/4] (1): 1
Select environment_manager:
1 - virtualenv
2 - conda
3 - pipenv
4 - none
Choose from [1/2/3/4] (1): 1
Select dependency_file:
1 - requirements.txt
2 - environment.yml
3 - Pipfile
Choose from [1/2/3] (1): 1
Select pydata_packages:
1 - none
2 - basic
Choose from [1/2] (1): 2
Select open_source_license:
1 - No license file
2 - MIT
3 - BSD-3-Clause
Choose from [1/2/3] (1): 2
Select docs:
1 - mkdocs
2 - none
Choose from [1/2] (1): 1
Select include_code_scaffold:
1 - Yes
2 - No
Choose from [1/2] (1): 1
```

After generation, initialise Git and commit:
```bash
cd zero-dose-burden
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit from CCDS template"
```

#### R Project
```bash
# Use the Associated Press R template
cookiecutter https://github.com/associatedpress/cookiecutter-r-project
```

**Terminal interaction**:
```
You've downloaded /Users/username/.cookiecutters/cookiecutter-r-project before.
Is it okay to delete and re-download it? [y/n] (y): y
  [1/5] full_name (Firstname Lastname): NDMC Alpha
  [2/5] email (): teamalpha@hackathon.org
  [3/5] project_name (New Project): zero-dose-burden
  [4/5] project_slug (zero-dose-burden): 
  [5/5] project_short_description (TK: short project description): Estimate district-level zero-dose burden by integrating household surveys and routine immunisation data.
```

After generation, initialise Git and commit:
```bash
cd zero-dose-burden
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit from AP R template"
```
:::

### Challenge 2 – Antimalarial Forecasting

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### Python
```bash
ccds
```
**Terminal interaction**:
```
project_name: antimalarial-forecast
repo_name: antimalarial-forecast
module_name: antimalarial_forecast
author_name: NDMC-Beta
description: Forecast district-level antimalarial medicine requirements and optimise allocation under supply constraints.
python_version_number: 3.10
dataset_storage: 1
environment_manager: 1
dependency_file: 1
pydata_packages: 2
open_source_license: 2
docs: 1
include_code_scaffold: 1
```
After generation:
```bash
cd antimalarial-forecast
git init && git add . && git commit -m "Initial commit"
```

#### R
```bash
cookiecutter https://github.com/associatedpress/cookiecutter-r-project
```
**Terminal interaction**:
```
full_name: NDMC Beta
email: teambeta@hackathon.org
project_name: antimalarial-forecast
project_slug: (press Enter)
project_short_description: Forecast district-level antimalarial medicine requirements and optimise allocation under supply constraints.
```
Then `git init` and commit.
:::

### Challenge 3 – Measles Alert Rules

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### Python
```bash
ccds
```
**Terminal interaction**:
```
project_name: measles-alert-rules
repo_name: measles-alert-rules
module_name: measles_alert_rules
author_name: NDMC-Gamma
description: Evaluate and optimise district-level measles alert rules for earlier and more reliable outbreak detection.
python_version_number: 3.10
dataset_storage: 1
environment_manager: 1
dependency_file: 1
pydata_packages: 2
open_source_license: 2
docs: 1
include_code_scaffold: 1
```
After generation:
```bash
cd measles-alert-rules
git init && git add . && git commit -m "Initial commit"
```

#### R
```bash
cookiecutter https://github.com/associatedpress/cookiecutter-r-project
```
**Terminal interaction**:
```
full_name: NDMC Gamma
email: teamgamma@hackathon.org
project_name: measles-alert-rules
project_slug: (press Enter)
project_short_description: Evaluate and optimise district-level measles alert rules for earlier and more reliable outbreak detection.
```
Then `git init` and commit.
:::

### Challenge 4 – Measles Transmission Modelling

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### Python
```bash
ccds
```
**Terminal interaction**:
```
project_name: measles-transmission
repo_name: measles-transmission
module_name: measles_transmission
author_name: NDMC-Delta
description: Model measles transmission and compare control and elimination strategies.
python_version_number: 3.10
dataset_storage: 1
environment_manager: 1
dependency_file: 1
pydata_packages: 2
open_source_license: 2
docs: 1
include_code_scaffold: 1
```
After generation:
```bash
cd measles-transmission
git init && git add . && git commit -m "Initial commit"
```

#### R
```bash
cookiecutter https://github.com/associatedpress/cookiecutter-r-project
```
**Terminal interaction**:
```
full_name: NDMC Delta
email: teamdelta@hackathon.org
project_name: measles-transmission
project_slug: (press Enter)
project_short_description: Model measles transmission and compare control and elimination strategies.
```
Then `git init` and commit.
:::
:::

> [Done] You have a complete project structure, tailored to your challenge, with a Git repository initialised and the first commit made.

---

## Viewing Changes – git diff

- `git diff` – shows unstaged changes.
- `git diff --staged` – shows staged changes.

```bash
# Modify your data file (e.g., survey data)
echo "Zone1, 2025, 0.12" >> data/raw/survey.csv
git diff
git add data/raw/survey.csv
git diff --staged
```

> Use `diff` to review changes before committing – good practice.

---

## Undoing Changes – git restore, git reset, git stash

- **Discard unstaged changes:** `git restore <file>`
- **Unstage a file:** `git restore --staged <file>`
- **Undo last commit (keep changes):** `git reset --soft HEAD~1`
- **Undo last commit (discard changes):** `git reset --hard HEAD~1`
- **Save temporary work:** `git stash`, later `git stash pop`

```bash
# Stash current work (e.g., an incomplete analysis script)
echo "WIP: incidence calculation" > src/incidence_calc.R
git stash push -m "WIP: incidence calculation"
git stash list
git stash pop
```

---

## Exercise: Practice Undoing and Stashing

**Goal:** Safely discard changes and stash work in progress.

```bash
# Create and commit a data loading function
echo "load_data <- function() {}" > src/load_data.R
git add src/load_data.R
git commit -m "Add data loading function"

# Make accidental change
echo "break" >> src/load_data.R
git restore src/load_data.R   # restore

# Stash a new exploratory notebook
echo "EDA for zero-dose" > notebooks/explore.Rmd
git stash
git stash list
git stash pop
```

> [Done] You can now safely undo changes and stash work.

---

## Branching – Isolate Your Work

- A **branch** is a separate line of development.
- Default branch is `main`.
- Use branches to experiment safely.

```bash
# Create and switch to a new feature branch (e.g., for model development)
git checkout -b feature/add_model
# Work, commit, push...
```

---

## Exercise: Branching and Merging

**Goal:** Create a feature branch, make changes, and merge back to `main`.

```bash
# Ensure main is up‑to‑date
git checkout main
git pull origin main

# Create a new branch for data cleaning
git checkout -b feature/clean_data

# Add a cleaning script
echo "clean_survey <- function(df) { ... }" > src/clean.R
git add src/clean.R
git commit -m "Add data cleaning function"

# Merge back to main
git checkout main
git merge feature/clean_data
```

> [Done] You have successfully used branches.

---

## Remote Repositories – clone vs fork

- **Clone** – creates a local copy of a remote repository.
  - `git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git`
- **Fork** – GitHub‑only: copy a repo to your own account.
  - Use to contribute without write access.

---

## Exercise: Clone and Push

**Goal:** Clone a repository, make a change, and push.

```bash
# Clone the team repository
git clone https://github.com/ndmc-davt/zero-dose-project.git
cd zero-dose-project

# Make a change (e.g., add a data source note)
echo " - Data source: DHS 2020" >> README.md
git add README.md
git commit -m "Add data source note"
git push origin main
```

> [Done] You have pushed a change to a remote repository.

---

## Collaboration – Pull and Push

- `git pull` = fetch + merge
- `git push` uploads local commits

> Always `pull` before `push` to avoid conflicts.

---

## Exercise: Simulate Collaboration

**Pair up:** One person creates a repo, the other forks/clones.

1. **Owner** creates repo on GitHub, adds Collaborator.
2. **Collaborator** clones, makes change, pushes.
3. **Owner** pulls and merges.
4. Switch roles.

> This is the core of team Git workflow.

---

## Handling Merge Conflicts

- Conflict: same file, same lines, different changes.
- Git marks conflict with `<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>`.

```text
<<<<<<< HEAD
method = "mean"
=======
method = "median"
>>>>>>> branch-name
```

Resolve: edit file, remove markers, then:
```bash
git add <file>
git commit -m "Resolved conflict"
```

---

## Exercise: Create and Resolve a Conflict

**Setup:** Two people (or two branches) modify the same line in a script.

- **Branch A:** changes line 1 to `method = "mean"`.
- **Branch B:** changes line 1 to `method = "median"`.
- Merge A into main, then try to merge B – conflict occurs.
- Resolve by picking one method or combining.

> [Done] You have resolved a real conflict.

---

## Advanced: git rebase (vs merge)

- `rebase` rewrites commit history to appear linear.
- Use **only on local branches** before pushing.

```bash
git checkout feature
git rebase main    # move feature commits on top of main
```

- Then fast-forward merge: `git checkout main; git merge feature`

> **Caution:** Do not rebase shared branches – use merge for public history.

---

## Tags – Marking Releases

- Lightweight vs annotated tags.
- `git tag v1.0` (lightweight)
- `git tag -a v1.0 -m "Release version 1.0"`
- Push tags: `git push origin --tags`

```bash
# Create annotated tag after completing a milestone (e.g., data ingestion done)
git tag -a v0.1 -m "Data ingestion pipeline complete"
git push origin v0.1
```

> Tags are great for marking milestones (e.g., submission deadline).

---

## .gitignore – Ignoring Unwanted Files

Prevent committing temporary, large, or sensitive files.

```gitignore
# Python
__pycache__/
*.pyc
.venv/

# R
.Rproj.user
.Rhistory
.RData
renv/library/

# Jupyter
.ipynb_checkpoints/

# Data
*.csv
*.nc
*.h5

# IDE
.vscode/
.idea/
.DS_Store
```

> Add `.gitignore` early and commit it.

---

## Large File Management – Git LFS

**The Problem:** Git stores every version of every file. Large files (datasets, models, images) bloat the repository, make clones slow, and can exceed GitHub's 100 MB file limit.

**The Solution:** Git Large File Storage (LFS) replaces large files with small text pointers, storing the actual content on a separate server.

**Installing Git LFS:**
```bash
# Linux (Ubuntu/Debian)
sudo apt install git-lfs

# macOS
brew install git-lfs

# Windows
# Download from https://git-lfs.com
```

**Initialise LFS in your repository:**
```bash
git lfs install
```

**Track large file types:**
```bash
# Track all CSV, NetCDF, and HDF5 files
git lfs track "*.csv"
git lfs track "*.nc"
git lfs track "*.h5"

# Track model files
git lfs track "*.pkl"
git lfs track "*.joblib"

# Track large images
git lfs track "*.tif"
git lfs track "*.png"
```

**Commit the tracking configuration:**
```bash
git add .gitattributes
git commit -m "Track large files with Git LFS"
```

**Push as usual:**
```bash
git push origin main
```

> **Note:** GitHub has a free LFS quota (1 GB storage, 1 GB bandwidth per month). For larger datasets, consider DVC.

---

## DVC – Data Version Control (Advanced)

- **DVC** is a tool that extends Git to handle large datasets and model files.
- It stores data on cloud storage (S3, GCS, Azure) or local drives, while Git tracks small metadata files.

**Install DVC:**
```bash
pip install dvc
```

**Initialise DVC:**
```bash
dvc init
```

**Track data:**
```bash
dvc add data/raw/dataset.csv
git add data/raw/dataset.csv.dvc
git commit -m "Add dataset with DVC"
```

**Push data to remote storage:**
```bash
dvc remote add -d myremote s3://my-bucket/data
dvc push
```

> **When to use:** Git LFS for files up to a few GB, DVC for very large datasets (10+ GB) or when you need versioned pipelines.

---

## Exercise: Large File Management

**Goal:** Set up Git LFS and track a large file.

```bash
# 1. Install Git LFS (if not already)
git lfs install

# 2. Create a dummy large file (e.g., 200 MB) simulating a climate dataset
dd if=/dev/zero of=data/raw/raw_climate_data.nc bs=1M count=200

# 3. Track NetCDF files with LFS
git lfs track "*.nc"

# 4. Stage and commit
git add .gitattributes data/raw/raw_climate_data.nc
git commit -m "Add large climate dataset with LFS"

# 5. Push (LFS files will be uploaded separately)
git push origin main
```

> [Done] You have successfully added a large file using Git LFS.

---

## GitHub Issues – Tracking Tasks

- Use Issues to track bugs, features, or tasks.
- Each issue has a number, labels, milestones, and assignees.
- Link commits with `#issue-number` to automatically close.

```bash
git commit -m "Fix bug #42"
git push
# When merged, issue #42 auto-closes.
```

> Issues are essential for team coordination.

---

## GitHub Projects – Kanban Board

- Create a Project board (e.g., "Hackathon Tasks").
- Add columns: To Do, In Progress, Done.
- Link issues to cards.
- Visualise progress at a glance.

> Great for managing hackathon workflow.

---

## GitHub Actions – CI/CD

- Automate testing, linting, deployment.
- Define workflows in `.github/workflows/*.yml`.

Example Python test workflow:
```yaml
name: CI
on: [push]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - name: Run tests
        run: pytest
```

> Set up Actions to ensure code quality automatically.

---

## Exercise: GitHub Issues and Projects

**Goal:** Create an issue and a project board.

1. On your GitHub repo, go to **Issues** → New issue.
2. Create an issue: "Add data validation function" (or similar for your challenge).
3. Go to **Projects** → New project (Kanban).
4. Add the issue to the "To Do" column.
5. Assign yourself and a milestone (optional).

> This is how you manage tasks collaboratively.

---

## Environment Management – Using uv (Python) and renv (R)

After installing `uv` (earlier), here is how to use it in your project.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Python (uv)
**Initialise a project:**
```bash
cd your_project
uv init
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate   # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
```

**Add dependencies:**
```bash
uv add pandas numpy scikit-learn
uv lock                # creates uv.lock – commit this!
```

**When a teammate clones:**
```bash
uv sync                # installs exact versions from uv.lock
```

> Commit `pyproject.toml` and `uv.lock` to Git.

### R (renv)
**Install `renv` (once per machine):**
```r
install.packages("renv")
```

**Initialise a project:**
```r
renv::init()   # creates renv.lock and renv/activate.R
```

**Add a package:**
```r
renv::install("dplyr")
renv::snapshot()   # updates renv.lock
```

**When a teammate clones:**
```r
renv::restore()    # installs packages from renv.lock
```

> Commit `renv.lock` and `renv/.gitignore` to Git.
:::

---

## Project Structure – CCDS (Cross‑Language)

A clean, reproducible project structure works for both Python and R. The AP R template generates a slightly different structure (with `analysis/`, `etl/`, `publish/`, `viz/`), but it is equally valid. If you prefer the standard CCDS layout, use the manual alternative.

```text
project-name/
├── checkpoints/          # Model checkpoints (if applicable)
├── data/
│   ├── raw/              # Original, immutable data
│   └── processed/        # Cleaned, model‑ready data
├── docs/                 # Project documentation
├── notebooks/            # Jupyter (Python) or R Markdown/Quarto
├── src/                  # Reusable functions (Python) or R/
├── scripts/              # Analysis pipelines
├── tests/                # Unit tests
├── models/               # Saved model artifacts
├── reports/              # Generated reports, figures
├── pyproject.toml        # Python (uv)
├── requirements.txt      # Python fallback
├── renv.lock             # R (renv)
├── README.md
└── .gitignore
```


## Branching Architecture
### Coordinating Parallel Workflows with Main, Dev, and Subbranches

Never develop directly on production timelines. We isolate our codebase into an ecosystem of stable execution tracks and rapid-iteration subbranches:

* **`main`**: The pristine production baseline. Contains fully verified, peer-reviewed, deployment-ready code releases.
* **`dev`**: The ongoing compilation line. Merges stable internal features before prepping a public version bump.
* **`feat/*`**: Experimental subbranches. Isolated environments spun out from `dev` to prototype single components (e.g., analytical modules) without breaking the team baseline.



## Integrating via Git Merge
### Context Preservation and the Non-Destructive Timeline

When a development feature is complete and peer-reviewed, `git merge` brings those changes back into the parent track. 

* **Mechanics**: Git creates a specialized, dedicated **Merge Commit** node that binds the history of both paths together.
* **Value**: It provides an absolute audit log. The repository graph records exactly *when* an experimental subbranch was built and *when* it was joined back into `dev`.
* **Command Loop**:
    ```bash
    git checkout dev
    git merge feat-data-cleaner --no-ff -m "merge: incorporate completed data pipeline"
    ```


## Streamlining via Git Rebase
### Rewriting Commit Topography for Pristine Linear Histories

As teams scale, multiple intersecting merge loops can clutter the project timeline. `git rebase` solves this by picking up your local commits and re-applying them directly on top of the newest parent branch updates.

* **Mechanics**: It temporarily side-lines your feature updates, synchronizes your subbranch baseline with the current state of `dev`, and writes your modifications linearly on top.
* **Value**: Eliminates messy intersection lines. The final path appears as a completely straightforward timeline.
* **Command Loop**:
    ```bash
    git checkout feat-spatial-model
    git rebase dev
    ```


---

## Team Challenges – Final Project (Four Groups)

Divide into four teams. Each team tackles one challenge using the `main`/`dev` branch model.

| Family | Challenge |
|--------|-----------|
| **Alpha** | Zero‑dose Burden Mapping |
| **Beta**  | Antimalarial Medicine Forecasting |
| **Gamma** | Measles Alert Rule Optimisation |
| **Delta** | Measles Transmission Modelling |

**Instructions:**
1. One member creates a GitHub repo and adds all team members as collaborators.
2. Everyone clones.
3. Create a `dev` branch from `main`: `git checkout -b dev && git push -u origin dev`.
4. Each member creates a feature branch from `dev` (e.g., `feature/data-load`).
5. Work in parallel: write code, commit, push.
6. Merge features into `dev` (resolve conflicts).
7. When ready, merge `dev` into `main`.
8. Final repo must include:
   - Project structure (CCDS or AP template).
   - Environment lock file.
   - `.gitignore`.
   - `README.md`.
   - Issues/Project board.
   - Large files managed with LFS/DVC.

---

## Submission Success Checklist

Before final presentation, verify:

- [ ] Git repository initialised and pushed to GitHub.
- [ ] Environment lock file (`uv.lock` or `renv.lock`) committed.
- [ ] `.gitignore` excludes `data/`, `.venv/`, `renv/library/`, cache.
- [ ] Notebook outputs cleared (or not committed).
- [ ] Project structure is clean and organised.
- [ ] `README.md` explains the project and reproduction steps.
- [ ] Both `main` and `dev` branches exist and are pushed.
- [ ] At least one feature branch has been merged into `dev` (and then into `main`).
- [ ] Issues and project board used to track progress.
- [ ] Large files (if any) are managed with Git LFS or DVC.

---

## Git Command Cheat Sheet (1/2) – Local & Basic Commands

| Command | Description | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| `git init` | Initialise a new repository | `git init` |
| `git status` | Show working directory and staging status | `git status` |
| `git add <file>` | Stage changes for commit | `git add README.md` |
| `git add .` | Stage all changes in current directory | `git add .` |
| `git commit -m "msg"` | Commit staged changes | `git commit -m "Add data"` |
| `git log` | Show commit history | `git log --oneline --graph` |
| `git diff` | Show unstaged changes | `git diff` |
| `git diff --staged` | Show staged changes | `git diff --staged` |
| `git restore <file>` | Discard unstaged changes | `git restore src/script.py` |
| `git restore --staged <file>` | Unstage a file (keep changes) | `git restore --staged data.csv` |
| `git reset --soft HEAD~1` | Undo last commit, keep changes staged | `git reset --soft HEAD~1` |
| `git reset HEAD~1` | Undo last commit, unstage changes | `git reset HEAD~1` |
| `git reset --hard HEAD~1` | Undo last commit, discard changes | `git reset --hard HEAD~1` |
| `git stash` | Temporarily save uncommitted changes | `git stash push -m "WIP"` |
| `git stash list` | List stashed changes | `git stash list` |
| `git stash pop` | Apply and remove latest stash | `git stash pop` |

---

## Git Command Cheat Sheet (2/2) – Remote, Branching, & Advanced

| Command | Description | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| `git clone <url>` | Copy a remote repository locally | `git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git` |
| `git branch` | List branches | `git branch` |
| `git branch <name>` | Create a new branch | `git branch feature/clean` |
| `git checkout <branch>` | Switch to a branch | `git checkout main` |
| `git checkout -b <branch>` | Create and switch to a new branch | `git checkout -b feature/model` |
| `git merge <branch>` | Merge a branch into current | `git merge feature/clean` |
| `git rebase <branch>` | Rebase current branch onto another | `git rebase main` |
| `git pull` | Fetch and merge remote changes | `git pull origin main` |
| `git push` | Upload local commits to remote | `git push origin main` |
| `git push -u origin <branch>` | Push and set upstream | `git push -u origin feature/model` |
| `git remote -v` | List remote repositories | `git remote -v` |
| `git remote add <name> <url>` | Add a new remote | `git remote add origin git@github.com:user/repo.git` |
| `git fetch` | Download remote changes without merging | `git fetch origin` |
| `git tag` | List tags | `git tag` |
| `git tag -a <name> -m "msg"` | Create an annotated tag | `git tag -a v1.0 -m "Release v1.0"` |
| `git push origin --tags` | Push tags to remote | `git push origin --tags` |

> Use `git help <command>` or `git <command> --help` for more details.

---

## Git Tips and Tricks

- **Commit early, commit often** – small, frequent commits are easier to review and revert if needed.
- **Write meaningful commit messages** – use imperative mood, keep under 50 characters, explain *why* not *how*.
- **Run `git status` before and after every command** – it prevents surprises and keeps you oriented.
- **Always `git pull` before you `git push`** – this avoids unnecessary merge conflicts.
- **Use branches for every new feature or experiment** – keep `main` stable; branch names like `feature/description` help.
- **Stash unfinished work** – use `git stash push -m "WIP"` to save work in progress, then `git stash pop` to restore.
- **Review changes with `git diff` before staging** – catch mistakes before they become commits.
- **Use `git log --oneline --graph` for a clear history view** – visualise branches and merges at a glance.
- **Add `.gitignore` at the start** – prevent accidental commits of data files, credentials, or cache folders.
- **Use Git LFS or DVC for large files** – avoid repository bloat and stay within GitHub’s size limits.
- **If in doubt, `git help` or `git <command> --help`** – the built‑in documentation is excellent.

---

## Using Git from RStudio and VS Code

### RStudio
- Create a new project from an existing directory or clone a repository.
- Use the **Git** pane to stage, commit, push, and pull.
- View history and diff changes with a GUI.

### VS Code
- The built-in Source Control panel shows changed files.
- Stage changes, commit, push/pull with one click.
- Extensions like **GitLens** add powerful history visualisation.

> Both tools work seamlessly with the same Git commands.

---

## Summary – The R & Python Hackathon Toolkit

- **Git** – tracks code, enables branching, works offline.
- **GitHub** – collaboration platform with Issues, Projects, Actions.
- **Git LFS / DVC** – manage large datasets and models.
- **Environment managers** – `uv` (Python) and `renv` (R) for reproducibility.
- **Project templates** – CCDS (Python) and Associated Press R template (R).
- **Branch strategy** – `main` for production, `dev` for integration, `feature/*` for new work.

> **Remember:** Always `pull` before you `push`, write clear commit messages, and use issues to track progress.

---





<div style="display:flex; justify-content:center; align-items:center; height:100vh; font-size:1.5em; color:red; text-align:center;">
  <h1>Happy Hacking!</h1>
  <h1>Thank You!</h1>
</div>


# References {.allowframebreaks frameoptions="shrink,squeeze"}

\tiny

::: {#refs style="text-align: left;"}
- Pro Git Book: https://git-scm.com/book
- GitHub Skills: https://skills.github.com
- uv Documentation: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/
- renv Documentation: https://rstudio.github.io/renv/
- Git LFS: https://git-lfs.com
- DVC: https://dvc.org
- Cookiecutter: https://cookiecutter.readthedocs.io/
- CCDS Documentation: https://cookiecutter-data-science.drivendata.org/
- Associated Press R Template: https://github.com/associatedpress/cookiecutter-r-project
:::
